issues.title: [Web]TextField text selection broken on Chrome
comments: OK, so the workaround is to use Safari instead of Chrome. I thought those days were gone :)
Everything is fine on 3.24.5

This problem is not unique to textfield. It's a common problem with SelectableText.rich() or many other text selection widgets on the web and it has been going on for chrome based browsers for almost years. The selection cursor jumps to places other than the selected area. However, when the selection is made and the mouse is released, we see that the selected area is actually selected. Also, once you try to make a selection and release the mouse, strangely the unrelated textfields above start to hover as if the mouse is over them. Once you hover and click on them, this problem is fixed until the next text selection. I'm leaving some example videos below.

Also these issues seem to be similar:
https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/162698
https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/163607

Native SelectableText.rich() example:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/85a2f4cc-37a4-4ef7-8dbd-daca0012d308

Some custom package re_editor usage example:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/79605503-c864-4815-bb50-6a9c4eef9a51

Seems to happen on chrome and chrome based browsers like Arc. Safari seems to be fine. I don't know about other browsers, I haven't tried them.

flutter: ">=3.27.3 <4.0.0"

issues.title: [web] ☂️ Enhancing Flutter Web's text editing system by using Platform Views
issues.body:
# Objective
One of our core goals is to make Flutter Web feel more idiomatic and natural on the web. A key part of this is to align the behavior of our text inputs to be more consistent with how text inputs behave on the web. This has major implications given how many impactful features branch out of a simple text box (spellcheck, autofill, etc) that web users are conditioned to expect to work.  So when text inputs don’t work on Flutter Web apps exactly the way they do elsewhere on the web, it has a glaring impact on UX.  

# Problems

### Clashing styles
We currently delegate all of the rendering of text inputs to the framework rather than the browser.
* When an `<input>` element is created, we apply CSS to make these elements and aspects of these elements (content, selection caret, selection highlight, etc) invisible.  By resetting the browser’s default styling, we enable Flutter to have full control over the look and feel of our text inputs.
* This has the potential to cause some issues via a “clashing” of styles.  The styles used to hide our text inputs don’t always work consistently across every combination of browsers and platforms, which can lead to strange rendering behavior.  For example, in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/99918, iOS Safari [does not support ](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/::selection)`::selection` pseudoelement styles, which is the method we use to make browser selection transparent. This leads to a clash of styles that creates two overlapping selections, which makes inputs not feel quite right in Flutter Web apps on iOS Safari. 

### Lazily rendered inputs
We don’t render `<input>` elements immediately.  They are created “as needed” when the user initially focuses on any given text input.  They also cease to exist when you blur. 
* This causes some complexity with autofill functionality. Without all of the inputs rendered immediately, we can’t leverage the browser’s native behavior to autofill multiple inputs on a page.  Custom logic has been added to enable autofill to work in Flutter apps today.
* Recreating the browser-native autofill behavior is no simple task.  This added complexity can create difficulties in the maintenance and debugging of autofill issues.

### Related issues
* **Selection**: 
   * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/79305
   * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/77076
   * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/94130
   * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/99918
   * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/70841
   * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/124483
   * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/134024
   * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/95958
   * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/138978
* **Autofill**: 
   * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/118337
   * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/66356
   * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/127694
   * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/125125
* **IME**:
  * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/138288
  * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/134797
  * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/134269
  * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/126066
  * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/98817
* **Other native behavior**:
  * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/40682
  * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/104082
  * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/96199
  * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/168322
  * https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/168588

# Solution
The above problems highlight an interesting relationship between Flutter and the browser.  In some instances, we are fighting against the browser (e.g. hiding `<input>` els) and in other instances we’re adding a lot of complexity to our text editing system in order to enable browser-native behavior to work (autofill).

An alternative approach might be to work with the browser to leverage its native behavior as much as we can within the context of Flutter apps.

### Platform Views
One way this could be accomplished is by using platform views.  Platform views allow you to embed native views in a Flutter app.  In the context of Flutter Web, we could use platform views to eagerly render `<input>` elements. 

Additionally, we would need the browser to take responsibility from the framework for the styling of certain aspects of the input:
* Content
* Selection highlight
* Selection caret
* Context menus / mobile toolbars


### Benefits
* By aligning the functionality of our text inputs more closely with the browser, we can better achieve idiomatic behavior of Flutter Web text inputs and all of the baked-in features that browsers provide for these inputs.  
* We can greatly simplify our codebase (especially in `engine`) by leveraging pre-existing browser features (e.g. autofill) rather than trying to recreate them.  A simplified codebase can help us improve our velocity, maintain our code more effectively, and debug issues more efficiently.
* We can fix browser-specific rendering issues for text inputs, some of which have no known workarounds in our current system.  By delegating the styling of content, carets, selections, and context menus to the browser, we can achieve a more native experience on a multitude of platforms. 

### Tradeoffs
* The framework does a great job of giving devs the ability to customize text inputs, potentially to a greater degree than the browser allows.  Delegating styling responsibilities to the browser comes at the expense of losing this greater degree of customizability.

### Potential issues / Unknowns
* Platform views are performance-optimized and will disappear outside of the viewport.  If `<input>` elements disappear outside of the viewport, this will break autofill functionality. 
* Could platform views cause performance issues in CanvasKit due to interleaving of `<input>`elements between `<canvas>` sheets? Is a page with many inputs feasible when taking into account this performance cost?
* Can we rewrite the text editing system in a way that continues to integrate well with the Framework’s code philosophy of being platform-agnostic?
* How will this work when semantics are enabled? Will this solution play well with semantics mode?
* Password autofill is currently broken for inputs within the shadowDOM in Chrome - but platform views need to be rendered within `<slot>`s and have to exist in the shadowDOM
comments:
This would be a major departure from Flutter's current approach.  You would lose Flutter's pixel-perfect rendering across platforms for text fields, and there would be a lot of work (and new ongoing bug reports) involved in trying to make it match as closely as possible.

I wonder how you might implement this in the framework side?  Maybe by recreating TextField, CupertinoTextField, and EditableText in your PlatformView and then dropping web support for everything lower-level than that (RenderEditable etc.).  Most Flutter users probably rarely go that low level, but it would break text editors like super_editor.  I'm not 100% familiar with how Flutter web currently handles this low level text editing stuff though, so fill me in if you have more ideas.

One possible argument in favor of using PlatformViews is issue https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/103163 on the iOS side of things.  Similarly to some of the points you made above, I currently have no good idea about how Flutter could solve that issue with our current Flutter-rendered approach.  CC @hellohuanlin.

I suppose to have the ability to make a decision on this, we might want to see a comparison of what things are not possible or feasible for Flutter to ever support with our current approach, and what things would we be giving up by using PlatformViews (maybe cross-platform support for everything below RenderEditable, etc.).

The fact that we would be moving away from one of Flutter's key advantages (fully controlled rendering) and taking an approach more like other frameworks like React Native makes me very wary of this idea.  However, if it's true that we are fighting a losing battle here, and there are important web features that Flutter is just never going to be able to support with our current approach, then maybe it's time we recognize our limitations and pivot.

CC @HansMuller @Renzo-Olivares @LongCatIsLooong 

Oh and by the way, there are some Pub packages that attempt to do text input via PlatformViews (on other platforms at least) such as: https://pub.dev/packages/flutter_native_text_input.

@justinmc Do we have anything documented about the need to be pixel perfect across platforms? AFAIK we are not pixel-perfect because we use different fonts on different platforms, so pixel-perfection ship has sailed. I haven't done UX research on this, but my gut feeling is that it is more important for text fields to feel natural to each platform, and because text fields behave differently across platforms, it also means they _should_ be different. We just need to carefully choose in what ways they should be different.

@htoor3 Do you think https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/58498 can also be fixed using the platform view design?
Also https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/40682

Thank you for your thoughts on this @justinmc! You are right; losing complete control of the rendering behavior is definitely the biggest tradeoff here.  

However, it's arguable that the pattern of issues that keep arising due to our current system are having a greater negative impact on Flutter Web users than losing out on this pixel perfect control.  Some of these issues have no feasible solutions due to the quirks and behaviors of certain platform + browser combinations.  

I've noticed this a lot with input rendering issues on iOS + Safari - sometimes Safari [just doesn't support certain CSS specs](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/::selection) and we're left with the choice of accepting poor UX for users in those cases or expending significant resources to find a viable workaround.  Oftentimes those workarounds add complexity to our codebase, which has negative longterm implications.

@justinmc thanks for bringing up `super_editor` as an example. I looked into it and they aren't using any of our text editing widgets or render objects. They only use `TextInputConnection` and `TextInputClient` to communicate with the engine. This means 2 things:

1. When we have a new web-specific implementation of `EditableText` using platform views, `super_editor` won't be affected.
2. We can't drop support for text input connections (i.e. `setClient`, `setEditingState`, etc).

As you said, most users go with `EditableText` or something higher level, so they'll get the new platform-view-based implementation for free with no breaking changes (except the fields will look slightly different). For the rare cases that need the low level input connection API, we can keep supporting them but we can simplify things a lot (e.g. remove the complex autofill support since these cases are usually doing rich text editors that don't need autofill anyway).

This proposal will allow us to play nicely with the browser and avoid a lot of hacks; and solve many issues that are unsolvable today.

Thank you all for the discussion here!  I don't think there's any killer requirement about pixel perfection down to the font level, it's just trading one set of bugs/weaknesses for another.  We'll fix the native fidelity bugs listed above, but we'll gain some related to inputs working/looking differently between platforms.  It seems like many of the problems we'd solve are severe and unfixable with the current architecture, and if the ones we'd gain are not, then that's a strong argument in favor of making the switch.

Maybe the next step is to write a proposal for how this might be implemented.  Then we could see more clearly what the limitations will be.

Also, +1 to keeping support for low-level text editing on web for advanced used cases!

As someone developing Web apps using Flutter and also maintaining a rich text editor plugin for Flutter (Fleather), I don't find using Platform View replacing Flutter's TextField a good idea.

If we are going to drop the whole text editing functionality on Flutter's side, a lot of projects would be killed.

If we are going to drop the `TextField`'s widget and rendering only and keep the text editing infrustructure to keep projects like `super_editor`, `fleather` and others working as @mdebbar said, we would still have most of those issues (#40682, #103163, #134024, ...) and have to fix them thus facing the said complexities anyway.

I suggest to have a complete rewrite of text editing infrustructure to better handle complexities and work on fixes for those issues. @htoor3

@Amir-P I agree. I'm working on Flutter Web Apps, and it's been a long journey. A TextEditor is an absolute necessity to complete web apps, and it's something every developer desires. Unfortunately, Flutter is currently unable to provide this. A TextEditor with basic functionalities should be included as a core widget in Flutter. Furthermore, it needs a complete redesign from the ground up. Additionally, the characteristics of languages other than English, such as two-byte characters in Korean, Chinese, Japanese, etc., should be considered from the beginning. I hope this can be given the highest priority.

Thank you for raising these points @Amir-P and @Patrick386 and I'd love to understand some of these concerns further.

We've gone through a few iterations, and the idea that we're leaning towards is _not_ to completely drop the `TextField` widget or a create a new `EditableText` for web, but rather integrate the platform view within the current system in a way that allows us to harmonize the strengths of the browser and the framework for web contexts.  We want to allow the browser to handle text rendering, selection, context menu, caret rendering, keyboard shortcuts - and continue to allow the framework to handle everything else (which includes rendering things around the content box). 

Ideally, this can lead to a more expected text input user experience on Flutter Web apps.  The goal is to achieve text input UX parity with standard web apps - leveraging the browser would help with this. Autofill on page load (without explicitly focusing), password managers correctly working, spellcheck, selection on mobile, internationalization etc are all fundamental web experiences that this system would improve.  Not to mention all of the current difficult-to-solve bugs linked in this issue would have a solution. 

On the flip side, we want these gains to be readily and painlessly available to Flutter devs and we're definitely keeping backwards compatibility and dev convenience in mind for the design - the last thing we want to do is break current user apps or drastically change existing APIs. For devs, this means you can render a `TextField` or an `AutofillGroup` as before and get the above gains for free.

I'm not as familiar with the rich text editor ecosystem - what are your concerns and what do you think this system would break for existing projects? I would love to keep these in mind as we flesh out the design.

For devs, this means you can render a `TextField` or an `AutofillGroup` as before and get the above gains for free.

I understand, and it's highly ideal. It's great to hear that all the issues could be resolved. In that case, I believe there's no need to overly consider backward compatibility. Making too many concessions for compatibility might hinder embracing such a significant improvement. Many developers will recognize this and be willing to embrace it for greater benefits. I'd like to see it happen as soon as possible. There's no need to hesitate; executing and addressing the issues promptly is what's needed right now. Rich text editors are indeed different, and therefore, should be offered as a separate alternative. Thank you!

@htoor3 Thanks for your response and the proposal.

I believe there may be some misunderstanding on my part. To clarify, I've summarized what I understood from your suggestion and outlined my concerns regarding them.

Based on your suggestion, the solution to the challenging issues we're currently facing, is to take advantage of Web's `<input>` element or iOS's `UITextField` instead of rendering our own input field. I can see how this would help with certain problems on Web or iOS, such as out-of-sync scrolling state on Web, paste button in context menu asking user permission issue on iOS, selection color on Web running on iOS and etc.

The primary objective of this significant change is to avoid the complexity of fixing these issues, simplify the codebase, and align our text input functionality with the browser's behavior.

However, considering custom rendering required by rich text editors, we won't be able to fully utilize native input elements, or at least they won't be visible and responsible for rendering, as is currently the case on the Web. So we're going to have those issues all over again and eventually, we're going to have to fix all those issues without taking advantage of native platform input elements and we're not going to have simplified codebase.

All this being said, I'm worried that the extensive effort required for this huge change, may result in even more complex codebase and slower and less prioritized bug fixes for cases not using `EditableText`.
Another issue this would solve: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/138288

For those interested, here is a [proposal](https://flutter.dev/go/web-native-text-editing) to the design which includes links to a proof of concept and some evidence that this new system could resolve some of the issues being tracked here.

However, I do think we need to prioritize plugging the leaky bucket that the current system is causing, because some of these issues are basic, expected, and standard text editing experiences.  Continuing to add patches to a system that's already working against the browser is turning into a losing battle, as we have no control over what updates are introduced on different platforms and browsers, and whether those updates might break the system we have in place (this has already happened a few times).  I will bring the RTE concerns to the team to see if we can discuss some kind of resolution or roadmap in place to support these use cases.

Text fields are one of the most problematic flutter parts. They are too integrated to environment on all platforms. I believe it will be better to use platform view for them as proposed. Every time when I need to solve error related to text fields I feel that I am fighting with platform. It may be better to move away from the original dogma of full screen control to achieve better integration.
Is this proposal still being worked on now that the author left the flutter team ? Looking at **Text**  (rather than text fields) as a platform view could also yield some benefits

I would like to express my sincere concerns and doubts regarding the lack of a definitive solution for a Rich Text Editor in Flutter Web, even after so much time. The Rich Text Editor is a basic function for any framework.

Currently, there are two popular alternatives for text editors in Flutter: quill_html_editor and html_editor_enhanced. However, as mentioned in the tutorial [Rich Text HTML Editor for FlutterFlow Apps](https://community.flutterflow.io/c/community-custom-widgets/post/rich-text-html-editor-for-flutterflow-apps-psYBqr7mpwcuonH), the latest versions of these packages present significant incompatibilities with FlutterFlow.

This situation places developers in a delicate position, as the absence of an integrated Rich Text Editor is a major obstacle for developing applications with advanced text editing requirements, such as blogs, copywriting, and generative AI applications.

I would like to know if there is a specific plan or roadmap for a definitive Rich Text Editor solution in Flutter Web.

Thank you in advance for your attention and continuous work to improve the platform. I look forward to a solution that meets the needs of the entire developer community.

Best regards,

issues.title: When `validator`of `TextFormField` widget returns an `AppLozalizations.of(context).translationKey` value, error message does not refresh with locale change
issues.body:
### Steps to reproduce

1. Create a Form in your Flutter App using a `TextFormField()`.
2. Localize your Flutter app according to [the docs](https://docs.flutter.dev/ui/accessibility-and-internationalization/internationalization).
3. Build simple form validation according to [the docs](https://docs.flutter.dev/cookbook/forms/validation), by returning a localized string via the `validator` (e.g. `AppLocalizations.of(context).myTranslation`).
4. Test the app using `flutter run`, and provide invalid input and trigger validation.
5. This will show an according error message displayed in the current app locale.
6. Trigger a locale language switch in your app (we're currently doing this using a provider to whose value the `locale` property of the `MaterialApp` / `CupertinoApp` listens to).

### Expected results

The locale should be switched in the entire app, to the language to which the locale switch occurred. 

### Actual results

Every string present in the app via `AppLocalizations.of(context)...` changes, except for the strings returned via the `validator` property in the `TextFormField()` widget & displayed in the way mentioned above before the language switch. Only a new call to `formKey.currentState.validate()` will refresh the validation error messages according to the newly selected language.

This issue has also been mentioned [on so](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/52217090/internationalization-problem-when-i-switch-languages), still without a really viable solution.

Yes, that's the solution I have implemented, but given the complexity of my widget tree, the code becomes much more complex than that. When I discovered `ScrollViewKeyboardDismissBehavior.onDrag` I thought that would be the default behavior on web. 

I think `ScrollViewKeyboardDismissBehavior.onDrag` might be smart enough to do this, or create a new one, something like `ScrollViewKeyboardDismissBehavior.onScroll` that has this behavior I'm looking for. What do you think?

Looks like this is an issue with scroll NotificationListener instead. I set `print` log at here and see `notification.dragDetails` is always null:

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/7b6d667701e38c1d9d700e2bcc29bc7a06598c59/packages/flutter/lib/src/widgets/single_child_scroll_view.dart#L269-L280

```dart
print('keyboardDismissBehavior: $keyboardDismissBehavior');
if (keyboardDismissBehavior == ScrollViewKeyboardDismissBehavior.onDrag) {
  scrollable = NotificationListener<ScrollUpdateNotification>(
    child: scrollable,
    onNotification: (ScrollUpdateNotification notification) {
      final FocusScopeNode currentScope = FocusScope.of(context);
      print('scope: ${notification.dragDetails} - ${currentScope.hasPrimaryFocus} - ${currentScope.hasFocus}');
      if (notification.dragDetails != null && !currentScope.hasPrimaryFocus && currentScope.hasFocus) {
        print('bingo!');
        FocusManager.instance.primaryFocus?.unfocus();
      }
      return false;
    },
  );
}
```

The output print log:
```console
keyboardDismissBehavior: ScrollViewKeyboardDismissBehavior.onDrag
scope: null - false - true
```

So `FocusManager.instance.primaryFocus?.unfocus();` will never be called on desktop platforms (this also happens on macOS app). 

Meanwhile, `dragDetails` is not null on mobile (Android):

```console

I/flutter (27163): scope: DragUpdateDetails(Offset(0.0, -1.5)) - true - true
```

@huycozy Thanks for the investigation. ScrollUpdateNotification.dragDetails is null when the scrolling was not caused by a drag. That's true in the case of desktop web, where you're scrolling with the mouse wheel or trackpad. So the behavior that you observe seems correct. When scrolling with a drag, a field should lose focus. When scrolling any other way, it should not lose focus.

I think the question here is whether or not the options should hide on scroll. @adonisRodxander do you expect the field to be unfocused as well, or would it solve your problem if the options hid and the field stayed focused (when scrolling without dragging)?

You can play around with a similar thing on the native web with <input> and `datalist`: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/play?id=ygeKaRZZOEalbnEsE34dNL6R3iA9NUIvmGwpPMLhgdMsZRMCyqt5H5y8%2BQeY0E5Nz0f6ceQGON7pCpPO

On scroll, it keeps the field focused, but hides the options. It seems to me like Flutter should probably make all desktop platforms behave this way including web.

@justinmc  Yes, hiding the options list solves my problem, but I still believe it's necessary for it to lose focus when scrolled. The code of `ScrollViewKeyboardDismissBehavior.onDrag`, removes the focus..

 `AutoComplete` by default uses a `TextFormField`. 

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/2d02215080e8cc36b4f5c8287f6935525fa3c643/packages/flutter/lib/src/material/autocomplete.dart#L154

The `TextFormField`, when used within a `SingleChildScrollView` with `ScrollViewKeyboardDismissBehavior.onDrag`, loses its focus. Therefore, if we only hide the options and keep the focus, we are not consistent in behavior, at least in my understanding. 

On one hand, we have a `TextFormField` that loses focus, and on the other hand, the `TextFormField` used in the `AutoComplete` that would maintain it, different behaviors for the same widget in the same situation.

> On scroll, it keeps the field focused, but hides the options. It seems to me like Flutter should probably make all desktop platforms behave this way including web.

@justinmc Yes hiding options is good enough when someone scrolls without dragging.
Fixed by #154675.

This thread has been automatically locked since there has not been any recent activity after it was closed. If you are still experiencing a similar issue, please open a new bug, including the output of `flutter doctor -v` and a minimal reproduction of the issue.
Reopening because https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/154675 was reverted in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/161277.

issues.title: Semi-transparent keyboard on iOS 26 reveals widgets that do not draw under it
comments: Thanks for the report. Seeing the same behaviour with latest SDK versions on iOS 26. Appears fine on iOS 18. ``` stable : 3.38.4 master : 3.39.0-1.0.pre-426 ```
+1 this is a huge polish issue, it's a super promninent UI issue that makes Flutter apps obviously non-native.
Any updates on this?
This also happens in a normal text field, from what I have seen. We need to get a hotfix for this ASAP Same issue here

This is the color of the modal barrier, now that the keyboard does not completely obscure the rectangular area at the bottom. This is going to require a somewhat large change, every bottom sheet widget may have to be updated how it handles `viewInsets` (I don't think there are many such widgets in flutter/flutter but a lot of packages will have to update their UI).

We'll have to change the definition / documentation of `MediaQueryData.viewInsets`, and update material/cupertino widgets like the material bottom sheet so they extend to cover the keyboard area instead of moving up to avoid the keyboard area.

I've routed this to the framework team's triage as per @LongCatIsLooong's investigation it appears the fix will need to be in bottom sheets. Please feel free to send it back to the text input team if needed!
FWIW, this is the UI hierarchy of the FlutterViewController when the soft keyboard pops up:

Also IIRC [Scaffold.resizeToAvoidBottomInset](https://main-api.flutter.dev/flutter/material/Scaffold/resizeToAvoidBottomInset.html) defaults to true. This may no longer be the most reasonable behavior in case the app has a floating widget that wants to stay at the bottom of the screen but above the keyboard.

Ah ok, so Flutter is still drawing under the keyboard? During triage I wasn't sure if the FlutterView even extended below the keyboard. 
We also discussed, this probably is not limited to just bottom sheets. The Scaffold for example can resize to avoid the bottom inset. We should check that as well.
Aha! @LongCatIsLooong too fast for me. :)

Quick look, there are about ~40 - note: included MediaQuery classes~ 25 uses (not actually individual widgets) of viewInsets in flutter widgets. I imagine there could be more cases than those accounted for here. I'll make a list and work through it.

- Scaffold.resizeToAvoidBottomInset - CupertinoPageScaffold.resizeToAvoidBottomInset - CupertinoTabScaffold.resizeToAvoidBottomInset - We should check use cases for SearchAnchor as well (--> _SearchViewRoute --> _ViewContent) - The ListView in _ViewContentState uses viewInsets to pad itself. These are the ones I found in my investigation today we should validate against in addition to those above. This will require a series of changes to several widgets and not something we would hot fix. Another case to consider as well, in some cases this will probably not be as simple as 'draw further below the keyboard', or to just fill the pace with the background color. Consider this, looking at the native contacts app: The keyboard is up. The text field is focused and in view. I can scroll the page (which dismisses the keyboard). You can see the rest of the page contents under the keyboard, the blurry green buttons and such. Currently, when the Scaffold resizes to avoid the bottom inset, if Scaffold.body contains a scroll view, the viewport itself ends up resizing to fit within the visible space the scaffold allots to it, instead of extending below the keyboard. Changing this viewport resize case might require further changes to things like getOffsetToReveal, showOnScreen, and ensureVisible, which are used to scroll things like a text field into view when the keyboard pops up. This should probably be a dedicated project for someone to tackle and investigate all the angles here. I am going to un-assign myself for now after having had a look, and will add to the queue for planning.

For planning as well: When we have this assigned and determine the course of action (and maybe even a workaround for the meantime) we should send out word to the ecosystem. There are likely other widgets in packages out there that need to account for this as well.

Actually, why not temporarily solve the problem by configuring this in the plist for the time being? ```xml UIDesignRequiresCompatibility ``` | false | true | | ------------------------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------ | | | |
Hey folks, has this work been prioritized? Given that [66% of all iOS devices are on iOS 26](https://developer.apple.com/support/app-store/), it feels like this issue is pretty significant

Hi @Lonely-ash, in the repro in the issue description the `showModalBottomSheet.backgroundColor` is set to `Colors.transparent`, but the modal child's Container is set to have a background color of `Colors.white`. Wouldn't setting both to the same color fix this issue? Or is the ask that this should be done automatically?
FlutterViewController after setting both background colors to the same color

I did not notice before that the provided samples are explicitly setting different colors (white and transparent). We think this is WAI, since if you do not assign mismatched colors, it works fine with one continuous color.
If anyone can reproduce in another configuration that is not explicitly set to different colors, please let us know!
Updated the note in the OP.

Setting the background color may work for cases where `resizeToAvoidBottomInset = false`, but if that option is set to true then Flutter just won't draw there. If you have a modal barrier, depending on the implementation of your dialog, your keyboard corners will have that color.

Additionally, this does not occur only for modal bottom sheets - it also occurs for normal pages that set `resizeToAvoidBottomInset: true` in the page scaffold.

Editable Text after clear has issue with buffer korean input
issues.body:
$ flutter --version
Flutter 3.7.4 • channel stable • https://github.com/flutter/flutter.git
Framework • revision b4bce91dd0 (3 days ago) • 2023-02-21 09:50:50 +0800
Engine • revision 248290d6d5
Tools • Dart 2.19.2 • DevTools 2.20.1

I have created a main.dart with the minimal steps to produce this issue but basically this might be related specifically to the way the Korean Keyboard has to build each character in a syllable. In a single syllable, the character can be made of 2 to 5 korean letters equivalent to the english alphabet. In the example syllable 광 (gwang), it is made up of 4 letters  (ㄱ ㅗ ㅏ ㅇ) but the keyboard has to keep a buffer of collected letters to build up to the full character. When the clear is called without clearing the buffer and breaking it, it seems to cause weird input until you deselect and re-select the Editable Text widget.

In the following modified Flutter Demo main project, you enter the text and when it is equal to the text to enter, the clear is called followed by the setState. From that point forward random characters are inputted until you unselect and reselect the editable text, clearing the buffer.

There are two methods in here, one producing the problem called onValueChangeWithIssue and the other not producing the problem called onValueChangeWithoutIssue. The only difference is that FocusNode.unfocus and FocusNode.requestFocus is being called. The problem with this fix is that on android devices, it produces undesirable behavior with the virtual keyboard and when a bluetooth keyboard is connected.

In the app I am building, I am not sure what the best workaround for this issue is.

class MyHomePage extends StatefulWidget {
  const MyHomePage({super.key, required this.title});

  final String title;

  @override
  State<MyHomePage> createState() => _MyHomePageState();
}

class _MyHomePageState extends State<MyHomePage> {
  final ValueNotifier<int> _counter = ValueNotifier<int>(0);
  final String _textToWrite = "이것은 한국말 된 문장이에요";
  final FocusNode _focusNode = FocusNode();
  final TextEditingController _controller = TextEditingController();

  @override
  void initState() {
    super.initState();
    _counter.addListener(onValueChangeWithIssue);
    //_counter.addListener(onValueChangeWithoutIssue);
  }

  @override
  void dispose() {
    super.dispose();
    _counter.removeListener(onValueChangeWithIssue);
    //_counter.removeListener(onValueChangeWithoutIssue);
  }

  void onValueChangeWithIssue() {
    _controller.clear();
    setState(() {});
  }
  void onValueChangeWithoutIssue() {
    _focusNode.unfocus();
    _controller.clear();
    setState(() {});
    WidgetsBinding.instance.addPostFrameCallback((ts)
    =>_focusNode.requestFocus());
  }

  void onChanged(String input) {
    if(_textToWrite == input) {
      _counter.value += 1;
    }
  }

  @override

    return Scaffold(
      appBar: AppBar(
        title: Text(widget.title),
      ),
      body: Padding(
        padding: const EdgeInsets.all(16.0),

          constraints: const BoxConstraints(),

            children: [

              Container(
                constraints: const BoxConstraints(),
                child: Row(
                    children: [
                      const Text("Enter Text:"),
                      Expanded(
                        child: EditableText(
                          minLines: 4,
                          maxLines: 10,
                          controller: _controller,
                          focusNode: _focusNode,
                          scrollController: ScrollController(),
                          autofocus: true,
                          autocorrect: false,
                          backgroundCursorColor: Colors.white,
                          cursorColor: Colors.black,
                          style: Theme.of(context).textTheme.headlineMedium!,
                          onChanged: onChanged,
                        ),
                      ),
                    ]),
              ),
              Text(
                'You have entered the text this many times: ${_counter.value}',
                style: Theme.of(context).textTheme.headlineMedium,
              ),
            ],
          ),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}

issues.title: Problems with TextInputAction.send TextInputType.multiline
issues.body:
很多三方的键盘[例如搜狗]， 设置之后之后
键盘上面 换行键 和发送键都有了。
但是发送键能正确使用， 换行键点击之后就无法换行了。 是一个空格的效果。
有办法能兼容这两个类型一起都是用吗？

像微信 和QQ等聊天工具都可以 换行和发送一起使用。

Many third-party keyboards [such as Sogou], after setting
Both the line feed key and the send key are available on the keyboard.
However, the Send key can be used correctly. After clicking the Line Feed key, line feed cannot be performed. Is the effect of a space.
Is there a way to be compatible with both types?
Chat tools like WeChat and QQ can be used together with line breaking and sending.

## Steps to Reproduce

`TextField(
          keyboardType: TextInputType.multiline,
          enableInteractiveSelection: true,
          maxLines: 4,
          minLines: 1,
          textInputAction: TextInputAction.send,
          onChanged: (value) {},
          onSubmitted: (value){},
)`

I don't know if there's an UIKit API to register a "return key pressed" callback, so currently it's implemented by monitoring if the software keyboard is trying to enter a `\n`: 

https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/659cdfc5a56840baed7f5481bbe4c22a173e851c/shell/platform/darwin/ios/framework/Source/FlutterTextInputPlugin.mm#L1283-L1324

**Update**  I installed the said IME on iOS and it inserts 2 carriage returns when you press "换行".

As a temporary workaround you could use a `TextInputFormatter` to turn carriage returns into line feeds:
```dart
FilteringTextInputFormatter.deny(RegExp(r'\r(?!\n)', multiLine: true), replacementString: `\n`);
```
@Rusino FYI, SkParagraph is treating `\r` as a soft line break instead of a hard line break(its `CodeUnitFlag` is `0x0006`).

issues.title: [Windows 11] Paste from clipboard history is broken
issues.body:
### Steps to reproduce

In Windows 10+, you can open your [clipboard history](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/tips/clipboard-history) using `Win + v`. This shows a popup with a list of previously copied content. Clicking on an item in this list should paste it. 

*Steps to reproduce*
On a Windows 11 machine (this does not repro on Windows 10):

1. Create a simple text field
2. Open the Windows clipboard history using `Win + v`
3. Click on an item to paste it

![image](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/assets/75003816/848f2725-7b9a-4d6b-9176-7ab0f8f6c1ae)

@dkwingsmt This looks like a keyboard issue in the Windows embedder. When you click on an item in the clipboard history popup, Windows:

1. Sets your clipboard to the content you clicked on
2. Synthesizes some win32 messages to simulate the `Ctrl+V` key presses

It looks like the Windows embedder does not convert these synthesized win32 messages properly into Flutter key events.
